title: Batt's Breakthrough: Prema Star Takes Jeddah Sprint as Porter Seizes Championship Lead
meta: Published: 18 April 2026 | By Samira Al-Rashid, Emerging Talent Scout | F1CC News
caption: Korv Batt celebrates his maiden F2 victory — a pole-to-win performance that ignites his title campaign.
body:
Korv Batt arrived in Jeddah as a driver with potential. He leaves as a Formula 2 race winner. The Prema Racing driver delivered a flawless sprint performance on the high-speed Corniche circuit, converting a maiden pole position into a controlled, composed victory that reshapes the championship fight. Batt, the soft‑spoken Singaporean with a Mercedes junior badge and a reputation for smooth, intelligent racecraft, held off Invicta Racing's Hamish Porter to win by 1.755 seconds. In doing so, he became the third different winner in three rounds — and thrust himself firmly into the title conversation. Mark my words: Korv Batt is no longer just a promising name. He is a contender.

Batt's journey to the top step has been one of quiet persistence. He grew up karting on the tight, technical circuits of Southeast Asia, far from the European powerhouses, before catching the eye of Mercedes at a junior shootout. In 2025, he finished eighth in his debut F2 campaign with Prema, a season of glimpses rather than glory. But Jeddah was different. From the moment he rolled out for sprint qualifying, the Prema driver looked dialled in. He topped SQ2 before delivering a stunning 1:29.812 in SQ3 to snatch pole by just 0.017 seconds from Porter. It was a lap of absolute commitment — three purple sectors, the car dancing through the high-speed sweeps of the Corniche circuit with the kind of precision that separates winners from nearly‑men.

Qualifying: The Surprises Begin Early

The sprint qualifying format produced drama from the outset. In SQ1, the biggest shock was the elimination of championship leader Ryan Melon, who scraped through in 15th — a result that set the tone for a difficult weekend. Even more stunning was the exit of Marek Dubišar, the Bahrain sprint winner and a driver who had looked imperious just a week earlier. The Invicta Racing driver could only manage a 1:31.760, placing him 19th and out of the session entirely. For a driver who had been second in the standings, it was a brutal reminder of how quickly fortunes shift in F2.

Alex Thompson, the Mercedes junior, was sixth in SQ1 and sounded optimistic: “Where did I go wrong? How was I to get into Q2 with 6th place from Q1 and yet come dead last in Q2?!” His confusion was understandable — his 1:31.782 in SQ2 left him last of the 15 runners, a sudden drop that even the Trident engineers could not immediately explain. The disappointment was etched on his face as he climbed out of the car, helmet still on, staring at the timing screen that had betrayed him.

SQ2 tightened the screw further. Melon, still struggling, was knocked out in 12th, leaving the championship leader without a sprint race point. The session belonged to Daniel Park, who topped the times with a 1:30.481, ahead of Marcos Olmedo and Porter. Ten drivers advanced to the pole shootout, and it was Batt who seized the moment. His lap was not a single burst of genius but a symphony of perfectly timed apexes, the Prema chassis riding the kerbs with the confidence of a driver who knew exactly what the car would do before he asked it.

Corentin Premier, who had taken a stunning pole in Bahrain, could only manage seventh. His radio message after qualifying captured his frustration: “P7 isn’t bad but we need to fix this car ASAP guys!” The VAR driver, usually calm and measured, sounded agitated — a sign that his machinery was not delivering what he knew it could. The mechanics set to work immediately, but the clock was against them.

Sprint Race: Control, Chaos, and a Maiden Victory

From the start, Batt held the lead into Turn 1, but the race was immediately shaken by an incident at the back. Tim Mittens, the Hitech driver whose consistency had been a hallmark of the early season, locked up heavily into the first corner and dropped to last. It was a moment that compounded a difficult weekend for the American, who would eventually finish 14th and fall further behind in the championship.

At the front, Batt built a small cushion over Porter, the Invicta driver matching his lap times but unable to find a way past. Behind them, the midfield was a restless swarm. Yann Asdelian and Kodiak Callahan duelled for third, their DAMS cars running nose‑to‑tail, swapping positions in a battle that would last for much of the race. Asdelian, the French Alpine protégé whose aggressive style has earned him a growing fanbase, made three attempts before finally getting ahead on lap 11. It was a pass that required patience and precision — qualities that Asdelian has worked hard to develop after a 2025 season marred by errors.

The race's most dramatic chapter came mid‑distance. Corentin Premier, running in the top ten, suddenly slowed. His voice crackled over the radio: “I’m losing power, guys.” The engineer responded calmly but firmly: “Sorry, but we have to retire the car.” Premier's reply was raw, unfiltered: “Why can’t I get a working car?!” He pulled off the circuit and climbed out, his race suit already unzipped, his disappointment visible even from the pit wall. For a driver who had taken pole in Bahrain and looked set for a strong result, the DNF was a gut‑wrenching blow. He stood by the barriers for a long moment, helmet in hand, staring at the track as if it had personally betrayed him.

With Premier out, the lead battle intensified. Porter, the championship chaser, made a decisive move on lap 9 to take the lead from Batt, his Invicta chassis looking racy on the medium compound. But Batt did not fade. He stayed patient, managed his tyres through the critical phase, and on lap 15, with the finish line approaching, he re‑overtook Porter with a move that was both brave and measured — a lunge into Turn 1 that left enough room for his rival but not enough to be challenged. From there, he pulled away, crossing the line 1.755 seconds clear. It was a victory built not on luck, but on maturity.

Behind the podium, the points positions were filled by drivers who had fought through the chaos. Charles Mounir took fifth for MP Motorsport, a result that continues his steady accumulation. Frederik Frid finished sixth, keeping Van Amersfoort Racing in the constructors' fight despite Premier's retirement. Ricardo Kaka, a guest entry for Rodin Motorsport, impressed on his debut with seventh place — a promising start for a driver making a rare F2 appearance. Daniel Park took eighth for Trident, Alex Thompson recovered to ninth, and Marcos Olmedo rounded out the top ten for Campos.

Championship Shake‑Up

The sprint result has reshuffled the drivers' standings dramatically. Hamish Porter's second place lifts him to the top of the championship for the first time in his career — 34 points, three clear of Melon, who failed to score. Batt's victory vaults him to third on 28 points, while Dubišar's SQ1 exit drops him to fourth on 26. Grunt, the Melbourne winner, is fifth on 25 after a quiet 19th-place finish in the sprint — a result that raises more questions about his qualifying pace. The field is now compressed like never before: the top ten are separated by just 24 points, and with the feature race still to come, Jeddah could yet crown a new championship favourite.

In the constructors' fight, Invicta Racing continue to lead with 60 points, but DAMS are closing fast with 45 after a double points finish from Callahan and Asdelian. Prema's 38 points — boosted by Batt's win — move them into third, while AIX Racing slip to fourth with 33. Rodin Motorsport, buoyed by Kaka's guest points, are fifth on 31, and Van Amersfoort Racing share that total in sixth. The battle for team supremacy is as tight as the drivers' contest, and every point in the feature race will count.

After the race, Batt was characteristically understated. “The car was perfect. I just had to keep it clean and not make mistakes,” he said. “I knew Hamish was fast, but I believed in my pace. This win is for everyone at Prema who has worked so hard over the winter. We're not done yet.” Porter, equally measured, acknowledged the close battle. “Korv deserved it today. I pushed as hard as I could, but he had the edge in the final laps. We'll come back stronger in the feature.”

The paddock now turns to the feature race, where the grid is set by a separate qualifying session. Jeddah's high‑speed, low‑downforce layout is unforgiving: one mistake can end a race, and the walls are close enough to punish the smallest error. With the championship as tight as it has ever been, the feature race promises to be a turning point. Batt has thrown down the gauntlet. Porter has seized the championship lead. Melon and Dubišar, the early‑season pacesetters, are suddenly on the back foot. The future is arriving faster than anyone expected — and it is wearing a Prema firesuit. Mark my words: this championship will be decided on nights like these, under the lights, with the walls closing in. And the drivers who thrive are the ones who will be remembered.
